summary: Jeff Atwood documents upgrading his custom-built PC by swapping in a new Intel E8500 CPU and overclocking it from 3.16 GHz to 4 GHz. He argues that the real value of building your own PC lies in the ability to incrementally upgrade components, something rarely possible with laptops or commercial systems. Through JavaScript benchmarks, he demonstrates that the 33% clock speed increase yields a meaningful 19% performance improvement. He maintains his controversial stance that quad-core CPUs are wasteful for desktop users since most applications can't utilize multiple cores effectively, making a fast dual-core with overclocking the smarter choice.
key_points: Building your own PC pays dividends through upgradability - you can swap components over time rather than replacing the entire system
Overclocking provides real, measurable performance gains: a 33% clock speed increase translated to 19% faster JavaScript benchmark times
Quad-core CPUs offer minimal practical benefit for typical desktop use since most applications are barely multithreaded
Sometimes you get lucky with upgrade paths - the E8500 was a drop-in replacement on a year-old motherboard without requiring new RAM or motherboard
BIOS flashing carries real risk - Atwood bricked his motherboard using Windows-based flash software and recommends boot CD or USB-based updates
money_quotes: In my opinion, quad-core CPUs are still a waste of electricity unless you're putting them in a server.
The economy of building your own PC also lies in upgrading it in pieces and parts to keep it up to date.
I overclocked my E6600 CPU from 2.4 Ghz to 3.2 Ghz, instantly increasing the value of the processor by about 800 bucks.
Compare the result of going from 3 GHz to 4 GHz with adding another two cores, which would produce exactly zero improvement in your JavaScript benchmarks.
Most apps are barely multithreaded, much less capable of taking advantage of all four cores.
Having four CPU cores won't help you much when they're all poking along at a leisurely 2 GHz.
Let this be a lesson to you, kids: don't trust Windows software developers!
key_insight: The true value proposition of building your own PC isn't just the initial cost savings, but the ability to make targeted upgrades over time that maximize performance per dollar spent.
